segments.text: Welcome to the show. I'm Lex Fridman and today I have a very special guest, someone who has shaped the AI industry more than almost anyone.
Thanks for having me, Lex. I'm Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA. It's great to be here.
Jensen, let's start at the beginning. You co-founded NVIDIA in 1993. What was the original vision?
We believed that there was a new kind of computing coming, one focused entirely on graphics and visual computing. Nobody else was doing it at that scale.
And at what point did you realize that the GPU could be used for something beyond graphics?
It was around 2006, 2007. We launched CUDA and suddenly researchers started using GPUs to accelerate all kinds of scientific computing.
CUDA changed everything. Do you remember the moment you knew it was going to be big?
Honestly the moment was when we saw the deep learning results coming out of Geoffrey Hinton's lab. The speedups were just staggering. We knew then.
Let's talk about the current moment. We're in what many people are calling an AI supercycle. How do you think about what's happening right now?
I think we're in a genuine platform shift. Not a bubble. The demand for computation is growing faster than our ability to supply it and that gap is widening.
Some people worry about the energy consumption of AI at scale. How do you respond to that?
It's a real concern and we take it seriously. But consider the alternative — the energy cost of not having better AI tools for drug discovery, climate modeling, all of it.
What does AGI mean to you personally? Do you think we'll get there?
I think the question of AGI is less interesting than the question of what we do with the intelligence we're building right now. We have systems today that are extraordinary.
Last question. What advice would you give to a young engineer who wants to work on the most important problems in AI?
Learn the fundamentals deeply. Mathematics, physics, systems. The people who will solve the hardest problems are the ones who understand things from first principles.
